---
title: "ABM Day 3"
subtitle: "Lecture 1: Recap, Latent Gaussian Models and INLA(bru)"
author: Lionel Hertzog
format:
  revealjs: 
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: 
      buttons: false
    preview-links: auto
    css: styles.css
---

```{r echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
## load libraries
library(tidyverse)
```

## What we saw so far

::: incremental
-   Day 1: fundamentals of Bayesian Data Analysis
-   Day 1: extension of regression models in BDA: mixed-effect models and regularization
-   Day 2: [...]
:::


## What we will see today

::: incremental
-   Introduction to Latent Gaussian Models and inlabru (now)
-   Adding temporal structure in models (later this morning)
-   Adding spatial structure in models (after lunch break)
-   Spatio-temporal structure and general discussion (to end this day)
:::

## Latent Gaussian Models - what we already now

Let's start from a simple random-effect model as already seen on Day 1:

$$
y_{ij} = \underbrace{\beta_0 + \beta_1 * X_i}_{\substack{\text{fixed} \\ \text{effects}}} +
\underbrace{u_j}_{\substack{\text{random} \\ \text{intercept}}} + \underbrace{\epsilon_i}_{\text{residuals}}
$$
With

$$
u_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u²); \epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\epsilon}²)
$$


## Latent Gaussian Models - what we already now

We can slightly rewrite this model with the following:

$$
y_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(\underbrace{\eta_{ij}}_{\substack{\text{linear} \\ \text{predictor}}}, \underbrace{\sigma_{\epsilon}²}_{\substack{\text{residuals} \\ \text{std. dev.}}})
$$
$$
\eta_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 * X_i + u_j
$$
What is **latent** - never directly observed - in this model?

## Latent Gaussian Models - A tale in three parts

LGM are made of three elements:

1. Observation parts, **important** not necessarily Gaussian
2. Latent fields, the joint Gaussian parts
3. Hyperparameters, used to draw the latent fields


## Latent Gaussian Models - Part 1: Observations

The first part in a Latent Gaussian Model (LGM) is the observation part, composed of a
likelihood function and a linear predictor (bridge to the latent part):

| Name | Likelihood | When to use |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Gaussian | $y \sim \mathcal{N}(\eta, \sigma^2)$ | Continuous measurements |
| Poisson | $y \sim \text{Poisson}(log^{-1}(\eta))$ | Count data, events |
| Binomial | $y \sim \mathcal{B}(n, \text{logit}^{-1}(\eta))$ | Presence/absence, proportions |



## Latent Gaussian Models - Part 2: the latent field

The latent field is the sum of all effects:

$$
\eta_i = \underbrace{\beta_0 + \beta_1 * X_i}_{\substack{\text{fixed} \\ \text{effects}}} + \underbrace{f_1(s_i)}_{\substack{\text{spatial} \\ \text{effect}}} + \underbrace{f_2(t_i)}_{\substack{\text{temporal} \\ \text{effect}}} + \underbrace{f_3(group_i)}_{\substack{\text{random} \\ \text{intercept}}} + ...
$$
Each $f_*$ is a latent component. For example a random-intercept is defined as:

$$
f_3(group_i) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{group}²)
$$

## Latent Gaussian Models - Part 2: the latent field

All latent quantities - that can be seen as coefficients to estimate - are grouped together into one vector $x$:

$$
x = (\beta_0, \beta_1, f_1(s_1), ..., f_1(s_n), f_2(t_1), ..., f_3(group_1), f3(group_2), ...)
$$

And the $x$ are drawn from a joint multivariate **gaussian** distribution:

$$
x | \theta \sim \mathcal{MVN}(\mu, Q(\theta))
$$
With $\theta$ the hyperparameters. Note that $x$ can be a very **big** vector (i.e. spatial fields).

## Latent Gaussian Models - Part 3: the hyperparameters

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="30%"}

[- The third and last part sets *priors* on the hyperparameters.]{style="font-size: 0.75em;"}

[- For instance, in a random-intercept model, the latent component]{style="font-size: 0.75em;"}

[$$f(\text{group}_i) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\text{group}}^2)$$]{style="font-size: 0.75em;"}

[has one hyperparameter $\sigma_{\text{group}}^2$.]{style="font-size: 0.75em;"}

:::

::: {.column width="5%"}
:::

::: {.column width="65%"}

[We can set a prior on this hyperparameter:]{style="font-size: 0.85em;"}

$$\sigma_{\text{group}}^2 \sim \text{Gamma}(0.01,\, 0.01)$$
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.height=3.2}
dd <- data.frame(x = seq(0.1, 5, length.out = 100),
                 y = dgamma(seq(0.1, 5, length.out = 100), 0.01, 0.01))
ggplot(dd, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_path() +
  labs(x = expression(sigma["group"]^2), y = "Prior density") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(text = element_text(size = 16))
```

:::
::::


## The general intuition

In the framework of LGM, space and/or time components are just additional latent fields to estimate. 

```{r echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# fig of space/time components
library(ggplot2)
library(INLA)  # for the Matérn field simulation

# ── 1. Random Walk ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(42)
n_time <- 200

# RW2: cumsum twice gives a smoother, trend-like process
innovations <- rnorm(n_time, mean = 0, sd = 0.1)
rw2 <- cumsum(cumsum(innovations))

# Centre and scale to keep it on a reasonable y-axis range
rw2 <- rw2 / sd(rw2)

df_rw <- data.frame(
  t = 1:n_time,
  y = rw2
)

p_rw <- ggplot(df_rw, aes(x = t, y = y)) +
  geom_line(color = "#7D3C98", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Temporal effect",
    x = "Time",
    y = expression(f[2](t))
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    text             = element_text(size = 16),
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

# ── 2. Matérn Spatial Field ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Build a regular grid
grid_size <- 60
coords <- expand.grid(
  x = seq(0, 1, length.out = grid_size),
  y = seq(0, 1, length.out = grid_size)
)

# Create SPDE object on the grid points as mesh
mesh <- inla.mesh.2d(
  loc      = coords,
  max.edge = c(0.08, 0.2),
  cutoff   = 0.02
)

# Build SPDE with Matérn covariance (smoothness nu = 1, i.e. alpha = 2)
spde <- inla.spde2.matern(mesh = mesh, alpha = 2)

# Sample one realisation from the Matérn field
Q <- inla.spde2.precision(spde, theta = c(log(0.5), log(1.5)))
field_sample <- inla.qsample(n = 1, Q = Q)[, 1]

# Project mesh values onto the regular grid for plotting
A_proj <- inla.spde.make.A(mesh = mesh, loc = as.matrix(coords))
coords$z <- as.vector(A_proj %*% field_sample)

p_matern <- ggplot(coords, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = z)) +
  geom_raster() +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "RdYlBu", name = expression(f[1](s))) +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(
    title = "Spatial effect",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    text         = element_text(size = 16),
    plot.title   = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid   = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks   = element_blank(),
    axis.text    = element_blank()
  )

# ── 3. Combine ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(patchwork)
p_rw + p_matern
```

## INLA

To fit these models we will rely on the package INLA because:

-   It is a Bayesian approach - remember priors, posteriors [...]
-   It is fast - no MCMC procedure, but based on numerical approximation
-   It implements numerous options for temporal / spatial models



## inlabru vs INLA

-  inlabru is a high-level interface built on top of INLA.
-  everything that is possible in INLA is possible in inlabru but not inversely (i.e. LGCP models)
-  inlabru implements some classical methods to do post-processing work on models

![](inlabru.png)

## inlabru basics

The classical model syntax for a mixed-effect model in R is:

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}
m_stanarm <- stan_lmer(uptake ~ conc + (1 | Plant),
                       data = CO2)
```

In inlabru the same model is written slightly differently:

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}

m_bru <- bru(uptake ~ Intercept(1) + conc + plant_eff(Plant, model = "iid"),
              data = CO2)
```

## inlabru basics

More generally the formula syntax of inlabru is:

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}
bru(y ~ latent1(x1, model = "aaa") + latent2(x2, model = "bbb"))
```

Where *latent1* and *latent2* are arbitrary names given by the user (it can be anything), and the *model* is one from the list of:

```{r echo=TRUE}
INLA::inla.list.models(section = "latent")
```


## Priors in INLA(bru)

INLA offers a wide range of prior distribution, see:

```{r echo=TRUE}
INLA::inla.list.models(section = "prior")
```


## Priors in INLA(bru)

Particularly interesting among these are the penalized complexity (PC) priors that penalized departure from an expected value.

In mixed-models these priors can be used for the deviation of the hierarchical terms in such a way:
$$
P(\sigma_{group} > u) = \alpha
$$

with $u$ and $\alpha$ two parameters set by the user.

## Priors in INLA(bru)

Here are some examples of PC priors density distributions $P(\sigma > u) = \alpha$ for different values of $u$ and $\alpha$:


```{r echo=FALSE}

sd_to_prec <- function(sigma) 1 / sigma^2
prec_to_sd <- function(tau) 1 / sqrt(tau)

params <- expand.grid(u = c(0.5, 1, 10),
                      alpha = c(0.001, 0.01, 0.1))

sds <- seq(0.01, 30, length.out = 100)
out <- NULL
for(i in 1:nrow(params)){
  dprec <- INLA::inla.pc.dprec(sd_to_prec(sds),
                         u = params[i, "u"],
                         alpha = params[i, "alpha"])
  
  
  out <- rbind(out, data.frame(sd = sds,
                               dprec = dprec,
                               u = params[i, "u"],
                               alpha = params[i, "alpha"]))  
}

ggplot(subset(out, dprec > 1e-10), aes(x=sd, y=dprec, colour = factor(alpha))) +
  geom_path() +
  facet_wrap(vars(u), scales="free",labeller = "label_both") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(text = element_text(size = 15)) +
  labs(color = "alpha",
       x = "Standard deviation",
       y = "Density")

```

## Priors in inalbru

Priors can be added to each latent parts separately via the *hyper* argument.

Adding a PC prior for a mixed-effect models with the following $P(\sigma > 10) = 0.01$ can be coded so:

```{r eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}
m_bru <- bru(uptake ~ Intercept(1) + conc +
               plant_eff(Plant, model = "iid",
                         hyper = list(prec = list(prior = "pc.prec",
                                                  param = c(10, 0.01)))),
              data = CO2)
```


## Model outputs

```{r echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(inlabru)
data(CO2)
```

```{r echo=TRUE}
m_bru <- bru(uptake ~ Intercept(1) + conc +
               plant_eff(Plant, model = "iid",
                         hyper = list(prec = list(prior = "pc.prec",
                                                  param = c(10, 0.01)))),
              data = CO2)

summary(m_bru)
```

## Coding time

A first plunge into inlabru
